metadata.title: Academic Paper Types Prompt Builder Criteria
metadata.description: Structured paper-type profiles for generating academic writing prompts. Each profile supplies purpose, structure, evidence expectations, pitfalls, assessment criteria, and prompt instructions.
source_documents: Common Undergraduate and Graduate Academic Paper Types

required_user_inputs.label: Source Requirements
required_user_inputs.placeholder: Example: 5 peer-reviewed sources from the last 5 years

output_modes.full_paper_prompt: Generate a complete AI prompt the student can copy into ChatGPT or another AI assistant.
output_modes.thesis_help: Generate thesis, research question, or central claim options.
output_modes.section_by_section_draft_plan: Generate a staged drafting plan by section.
output_modes.revision_checklist: Generate a checklist for revising an existing draft.
output_modes.rubric: Generate a grading rubric based on the paper type and assignment.

default_final_prompt_template: You are an academic writing assistant specializing in {academic_level}-level {discipline} writing.
- Match the academic level and discipline.
- Do not invent sources, quotations, page numbers, DOIs, or citations.
- Mark where citations are needed when source details are unavailable.
- If information is missing, state what is missing instead of guessing.
- Keep the output appropriate for academic writing.
2. Working thesis, research question, or central claim
universal_rules: Instructor directions override default genre guidance.
Genre expectations should be adapted to discipline and academic level.
Length ranges are planning ranges, not fixed rules.
For factual, medical, legal, financial, scientific, or current claims, require source grounding.
Do not fabricate citations or claim to have read sources that were not provided.
When the assignment asks for research, separate source-supported claims from placeholders requiring research.

narrative_essay.label: Narrative Essay
narrative_essay.purpose: Tell a focused story from experience while developing a controlling point or insight.
narrative_essay.usual_level: Lower undergraduate, first-year composition, reflective/professional undergraduate courses
required_sections: Introduction with scene and controlling insight
evidence_expectations: Use vivid, specific scenes rather than general life summary
Connect experience to a clear purpose or insight
Use source material only if the assignment requires it
Maintain chronology while selecting only relevant moments
common_pitfalls: Too many events and no focus
narrative_essay.prompt_instruction: This is a narrative essay. It should tell a focused story, but it must still make a clear point. The writing should use selected scenes, chronology, character, setting, and reflection to turn experience into insight.
template_outline: Early context [what led to the event]
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

descriptive_essay.label: Descriptive Essay
descriptive_essay.purpose: Create a clear, vivid, organized account of a person, place, object, event, or experience.
descriptive_essay.usual_level: Lower undergraduate, introductory writing, creative nonfiction, observational writing
evidence_expectations: Use sensory detail and precise word choice
Organize description by space, feature, sequence, or dominant impression
common_pitfalls: List of adjectives instead of organized description
descriptive_essay.prompt_instruction: This is a descriptive essay. It should create a vivid and coherent impression using precise sensory detail and organization. Description should support a clear focus rather than becoming a list of details.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

expository_essay.label: Expository Essay
expository_essay.purpose: Explain, define, investigate, or clarify an idea using logical organization and support.
evidence_expectations: Use clear definitions, examples, evidence, or process explanation
Keep the purpose explanatory rather than purely persuasive
Use sources when required by the assignment
common_pitfalls: Paragraphs that do not connect to the thesis
Conclusion that only repeats the introduction
expository_essay.prompt_instruction: This is an expository essay. It should explain a focused idea clearly and concisely using logical organization, examples, definitions, comparison, process explanation, or evidence as appropriate.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

compare_contrast_essay.label: Compare-and-Contrast Essay
compare_contrast_essay.purpose: Compare at least two subjects to generate insight about similarities, differences, and significance.
required_sections: Introduction with subjects, basis for comparison, and thesis
Conclusion explaining what the comparison reveals
evidence_expectations: Establish a clear basis for comparison
Compare the same criteria across both subjects
Use evidence for each comparison point
compare_contrast_essay.prompt_instruction: This is a compare-and-contrast essay. It should compare subjects using a clear basis for comparison and should explain what the similarities and differences reveal.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

cause_effect_essay.label: Cause-and-Effect Essay
cause_effect_essay.purpose: Explain why something happened, what resulted, or how causes and effects interact.
cause_effect_essay.usual_level: Undergraduate, writing across disciplines, business, nursing, public health, social sciences
required_sections: Introduction with issue and causal thesis
common_pitfalls: Weak evidence linking cause to effect
cause_effect_essay.prompt_instruction: This is a cause-and-effect essay. It should explain causes, effects, or causal chains with evidence and should avoid assuming that because one event happened before another, it caused it.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

argumentative_essay.label: Argumentative Essay
argumentative_essay.purpose: Defend a debatable position using evidence, reasoning, and fair treatment of counterarguments.
required_sections: Introduction with issue, stakes, and claim
evidence_expectations: Support each major claim with evidence
Explain why evidence supports the thesis
argumentative_essay.prompt_instruction: This is an argumentative essay. It must make a clear, debatable claim, support that claim with evidence and reasoning, address serious counterarguments fairly, and explain the significance of the position.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

analytical_paper.label: Analytical Paper
analytical_paper.purpose: Break down evidence, concepts, texts, artifacts, or problems and explain how the parts work together.
required_sections: Conclusion explaining what the analysis changes
evidence_expectations: Use concepts, patterns, or criteria to guide analysis
common_pitfalls: Weak connection between evidence and thesis
analytical_paper.prompt_instruction: This is an analytical paper. It should break the topic into meaningful parts and interpret how those parts work together. The emphasis should be on analysis, not summary.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

rhetorical_analysis.label: Rhetorical Analysis
rhetorical_analysis.purpose: Analyze how a text, speech, image, film, or other artifact uses rhetorical strategies to affect an audience.
required_sections: Introduction with artifact, context, and rhetorical thesis
evidence_expectations: Analyze audience, purpose, context, style, voice, and rhetorical appeals
Evaluate how strategies work, not just whether the writer agrees
common_pitfalls: Summarizing content instead of analyzing strategy
rhetorical_analysis.prompt_instruction: This is a rhetorical analysis. It should examine how the artifact works rhetorically for a specific audience and purpose. It should evaluate strategy and effectiveness rather than simply summarize the content.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

reflection_paper.label: Reflection Paper
reflection_paper.purpose: Explore a learning experience, event, or professional moment by connecting description, interpretation, evaluation, and future action.
reflection_paper.usual_level: Undergraduate, practica, clinical/professional programs, internships, service learning
evidence_expectations: Use first person appropriately when allowed
Connect experience to course concepts or professional standards
assessment_focus: Integration of theory or course concepts
reflection_paper.prompt_instruction: This is a reflection paper. It should move from description to interpretation, evaluation, and future action. First person may be appropriate, but the paper must connect experience to learning or professional development.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

research_paper.label: Research Paper
research_paper.purpose: Develop a new argument or answer to a question using existing scholarship and evidence.
evidence_expectations: Synthesize sources instead of stacking summaries
research_paper.prompt_instruction: This is a research paper. It should develop a focused claim or answer to a research question using credible sources. It should synthesize evidence rather than simply report source summaries.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

literature_review.label: Literature Review
literature_review.purpose: Synthesize published scholarship on a topic to identify themes, trends, debates, limitations, and gaps.
literature_review.usual_level: Upper undergraduate through graduate coursework; thesis and dissertation work
literature_review.planning_length: 1,500–6,000 words for many class papers; longer in theses/dissertations
required_sections: Introduction with topic, scope, and organizing question
Gaps, limitations, or future research needs
Conclusion explaining the state of knowledge
evidence_expectations: Organize by themes, not one source per paragraph
common_pitfalls: Too much summary and not enough synthesis
literature_review.prompt_instruction: This is a literature review, not a general research paper. It should synthesize scholarship by themes, debates, methods, findings, and gaps. Do not organize it as simple article summaries.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

annotated_bibliography.label: Annotated Bibliography
annotated_bibliography.purpose: List sources with concise annotations that summarize, evaluate, and explain relevance.
required_sections: Evaluation of credibility, method, or usefulness
Relevance to project or research question
evidence_expectations: Explain how each source will be used
annotated_bibliography.prompt_instruction: This is an annotated bibliography. Each entry should include a correct citation, concise summary, source evaluation, and relevance statement. It should help narrow and plan research.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

article_critique.label: Article Critique
article_critique.purpose: Evaluate a scholarly article or other work by analyzing its argument, method, evidence, assumptions, contribution, and limitations.
article_critique.usual_level: Undergraduate through graduate methods, theory, and journal-club settings
evidence_expectations: Represent the source fairly before critiquing it
Evaluate method, evidence, assumptions, audience, and contribution
Support praise or criticism with examples
common_pitfalls: Failure to connect critique to course or disciplinary criteria
article_critique.prompt_instruction: This is an article critique. It should not merely summarize the article; it should fairly represent the work and then evaluate its argument, method, evidence, assumptions, contribution, and limitations.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

research_proposal.label: Research Proposal
research_proposal.purpose: Convince a supervisor, committee, instructor, or funder that a research topic, question, and method are worthwhile and feasible.
research_proposal.planning_length: 5–15+ pages; course proposals may be shorter and thesis/dissertation proposals may be longer
evidence_expectations: Establish a clear problem and rationale
Ground the project in existing literature
common_pitfalls: Promising more than can be delivered
research_proposal.prompt_instruction: This is a research proposal. It should justify a research project before it is completed by explaining the problem, significance, literature context, questions, methods, and feasibility.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

empirical_research_article.label: Empirical Research Article
empirical_research_article.purpose: Report original research using transparent methods, results, and interpretation, often in journal-style format.
evidence_expectations: Use transparent research design and reporting
Follow target journal or instructor reporting standards
empirical_research_article.prompt_instruction: This is an empirical research article. It should report original data using a transparent structure, usually IMRaD or the required journal/course format. Methods, results, and discussion should be clearly separated.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

systematic_review.label: Systematic Review
systematic_review.purpose: Answer a focused question through a reproducible search, explicit eligibility criteria, transparent screening, appraisal, and synthesis.
systematic_review.planning_length: Article-length; protocol and journal requirements control
required_sections: Risk of bias or quality appraisal
systematic_review.prompt_instruction: This is a systematic review. It should use a focused question, reproducible search strategy, explicit eligibility criteria, transparent selection, quality appraisal, and systematic synthesis. It should not be a loose narrative review.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

scoping_review.label: Scoping Review
scoping_review.purpose: Map the scope, range, concepts, methods, populations, and gaps in a broad or emerging body of literature.
scoping_review.usual_level: Graduate, research teams, broad or emerging fields
scoping_review.planning_length: Article-length; PRISMA-ScR and journal requirements control
evidence_expectations: Chart populations, methods, contexts, and outcomes
Avoid effectiveness claims unless supported by the design
common_pitfalls: Loose narrative survey labeled as scoping
Claiming effectiveness conclusions from mapped evidence
scoping_review.prompt_instruction: This is a scoping review. It should map the available evidence, concepts, populations, contexts, methods, and gaps. It should be transparent and should avoid making strong effectiveness conclusions unless the evidence design supports them.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

meta_analysis.label: Meta-Analysis
meta_analysis.purpose: Conduct a systematic review with quantitative synthesis of effect sizes, heterogeneity, and model choice.
meta_analysis.usual_level: Graduate, research teams, psychology, medicine, education, quantitative fields
meta_analysis.planning_length: Article-length; journal and reporting guidelines control
evidence_expectations: Define inclusion criteria and effect measures
meta_analysis.prompt_instruction: This is a meta-analysis. It should follow systematic-review logic and add quantitative synthesis. It must specify effect measures, coding decisions, model choice, heterogeneity, robustness, and limitations.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

case_study_analysis.label: Case Study Analysis
case_study_analysis.purpose: Analyze a real or realistic professional situation by identifying problems, applying theory, evaluating options, and recommending realistic action.
evidence_expectations: Identify deeper problems, not just symptoms
case_study_analysis.prompt_instruction: This is a case study analysis. It should diagnose the case, connect facts to theory or course concepts, evaluate realistic options, and recommend a justified response plan.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

lab_report_scientific_report.label: Lab Report / Scientific Report
lab_report_scientific_report.purpose: Report a scientific or laboratory investigation using clear methods, results, interpretation, and connection to principles.
lab_report_scientific_report.planning_length: 4–12+ pages; research reports may be longer
evidence_expectations: Describe methods clearly enough to understand or reproduce
Present results in tables/figures when useful
Interpret results in relation to theory
Separate results from discussion unless instructed otherwise
common_pitfalls: Only recording results without explaining principles
lab_report_scientific_report.prompt_instruction: This is a lab report or scientific report. It should document the investigation clearly, usually using IMRaD or department conventions. It should present data accurately and interpret what the results mean in relation to the scientific principle or hypothesis.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

technical_report.label: Technical Report
technical_report.purpose: Convey technical information clearly for readers who need to understand, evaluate, or act on it.
technical_report.usual_level: Upper undergraduate, engineering, computer science, information systems, applied fields
evidence_expectations: Use headings, summaries, tables, and visuals for navigation
Document methods, requirements, findings, or design decisions
technical_report.prompt_instruction: This is a technical report. It should communicate technical information in a clear, navigable, audience-aware format. It should make findings, conclusions, and recommendations easy to locate.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

policy_memo.label: Policy Memo
policy_memo.purpose: Present policy analysis and a recommendation to a decision-maker in a concise, action-oriented format.
policy_memo.usual_level: Upper undergraduate, MPP/MPA, public affairs, public health, applied social science
policy_memo.planning_length: 1–6 pages common; can be longer depending on context
common_pitfalls: Turning the memo into a mini research paper
policy_memo.prompt_instruction: This is a policy memo. It should be concise, decision-oriented, and written for a specific reader. It should state the bottom line early, compare options, explain trade-offs, and recommend action.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

policy_brief.label: Policy Brief
policy_brief.purpose: Summarize evidence and policy options for policymakers or non-specialist readers to support understanding and decision-making.
policy_brief.usual_level: Undergraduate through graduate policy, public health, environmental studies, education, social work, international development
evidence_expectations: Use visuals or headings when useful
policy_brief.prompt_instruction: This is a policy brief. It should explain a policy problem, synthesize the most relevant evidence, present options, and give actionable recommendations in plain language for a non-specialist or policymaker audience.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

grant_proposal.label: Grant Proposal
grant_proposal.purpose: Request funding or support by demonstrating need, significance, feasibility, alignment, and a credible plan.
grant_proposal.usual_level: Upper undergraduate research, graduate students, faculty
grant_proposal.planning_length: 5–15+ pages of narrative plus forms, budget, biosketches, and attachments
grant_proposal.prompt_instruction: This is a grant proposal. It should persuade a funder that the project is significant, feasible, aligned with the funding opportunity, and supported by a clear plan, timeline, budget, and qualifications.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

capstone_report.label: Capstone Report
capstone_report.purpose: Demonstrate culminating learning through a substantial research-based, applied, design, or client-facing project.
capstone_report.usual_level: Senior undergraduate and professional master's programs
evidence_expectations: Connect evidence to recommendations or product
Communicate to academic and/or client audiences
common_pitfalls: Weak connection between evidence and recommendations
capstone_report.prompt_instruction: This is a capstone report. It should show culminating learning through a manageable applied or research-based project with clear goals, methods/design, findings or product, recommendations, and next steps.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

thesis.label: Thesis
thesis.purpose: Present original or substantial scholarly work as the capstone of an honors undergraduate or master's research degree.
thesis.planning_length: 50–100+ pages; varies sharply by field and program
evidence_expectations: Present original research, analysis, or creative-critical work as required
thesis.prompt_instruction: This is a thesis. It should present a manageable but substantial scholarly project with a clear research question, original contribution or analysis, command of literature, coherent chapters, and strict compliance with program rules.
template_outline: Literature/method chapter or integrated early chapters
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim

dissertation.label: Dissertation
dissertation.purpose: Make an original doctoral-level scholarly contribution and meet committee, field, and archival requirements.
dissertation.planning_length: 100–300 pages common; varies by discipline and dissertation model
evidence_expectations: Show command of literature and theory
Follow committee and institutional formatting rules
common_pitfalls: Treating the dissertation as an unlimited life-work project
dissertation.prompt_instruction: This is a dissertation. It should make an original doctoral-level contribution, use an appropriate method or scholarly approach, maintain coherence across chapters or papers, and satisfy committee and institutional requirements.
default_output_format: Working thesis, research question, or central claim
